exemplar.title: To what extent is Hannah Arendt's critique of the use of violence by social movements in the struggle for political and/or social change philosophically strong?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent is Hannah Arendt's critique of the use of violence by social movements in the struggle for political and/or social change philosophically strong?

strengths: Research question is clearly stated, wellâfocused, and consistently drives the discussion.
Abstract and contents page provide a comprehensive roadmap, ensuring topic is communicated effectively.
Appropriate range of primary and secondary sources with clear evidence of informed selection.
weaknesses: Minor drift into historical detail occasionally interrupts conceptual thread.
Methodological approach to source selection could be more explicitly detailed.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although the work showed minor ambiguities in detailing its methodology and occasional historical digressions, it consistently communicated the topic accurately and maintained a tight focus on evaluating Arendtâs critique. The research question was prominently stated and effectively connected to every section, and the methodological choices were well matched to the question. These strengths justify the maximum 6 marks for Focus and Method.

strengths: Consistently relevant and properly integrated use of Arendt, Fanon, and contemporary examples.
Clear, coherent presentation of philosophical knowledge with effective use of sources.
Accurate and consistent use of subject-specific terminology and concepts.
weaknesses: Occasional oversimplifications in theoretical claims (e.g., inevitability of regimes falling).
Working definition of violence could engage more deeply with established philosophical definitions.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The essay demonstrates a very strong command of philosophical material, integrating primary texts and case studies coherently while employing precise technical language. Minor conceptual simplifications prevent absolute perfection, but overall the application of source material and subject understanding fully meet the top band descriptors, warranting 6 marks.

strengths: Research is consistently relevant, tying primary and secondary sources directly back to the research question.
Analysis develops original frameworks (power-violence continuum; moral-agent model) with wellâsupported conclusions.
Argument is coherent and synthesizes multiple perspectives, demonstrating strong critical engagement.
weaknesses: Evaluation lacks systematic weighing of alternative perspectives and additional theorists (e.g., Sorel, Gandhi).
Some evaluative criteria (e.g., what makes a critique 'philosophically strong') are under-defined.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The essay presents goodâquality research and delivers wellâsupported, creative analysisâpushing beyond description into original synthesis. Its critique and reformulation of Arendtâs arguments show high-level critical thinking, though it stops short of fully systematic evaluation of all counter-perspectives. These attributes place it at the low end of the 10â12 band, justifying 10 marks.

strengths: All formal requirements (word count, title page, pagination) are met and citations are consistent.
Structure is clearly appropriate: logical sections, smooth transitions, comprehensive contents page.
weaknesses: Some long URLs interrupt the layout and header formatting is not entirely consistent.
Absence of visual aids (e.g., diagram of power-violence continuum) that could enhance clarity.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Presentation fully satisfies the IB requirements with an effective and reader-friendly layout. Minor formatting inconsistencies and overly long URLs do not impede readability. These strengths align with the highest band descriptors for Presentation, meriting 4 marks.

strandSelections.reasoning: The research questionââTo what extent is Hannah Arendtâs critique â¦ philosophically strong?ââis explicitly stated on the title page and repeated in section headings.  It is narrow enough to allow sustained analysis of one primary text (On Violence) while permitting engagement with a key counter-author (Fanon).  All subsequent discussion is tied back to evaluating the strength of Arendtâs critique, showing a clear and effective connection between the RQ and the essayâs argumentation.  Focus occasionally drifts to historical examples, but never so far as to undermine coherence.

Key philosophical termsâviolence, power, legitimacy, instrumental vs. intrinsic valueâare employed correctly and consistently.  The essay distinguishes consequentialist and non-teleological positions, uses âpolis,â âteleology,â âmeansâend,â etc., with accuracy.  Minor lapses in precision (occasional colloquial phrasing) prevent the maximum mark but do not hinder understanding.

The title immediately states the precise philosophical focusâan assessment of Arendtâs critique of violenceâand the introduction explains the key concepts (violence, social movements, power) and historical context that will be used.  The student clearly lays out the scope (Arendtâs arguments, counter-arguments, reformulation) and provides a detailed abstract and contents page, showing the reader exactly what will be covered.  Minor ambiguities remain (e.g., how âphilosophically strongâ will be measured), but overall the topic is communicated accurately and with thorough explanation, matching the upper band.

Formal requirementsâword count stated, title given, pages evidently numbered in the original document, and a complete bibliographyâare all met.  Citations appear in a consistent style and tables/sections are clearly laid out.  Occasional long URLs and minor spacing issues remain, but layout is essentially correct and fully supports readability, placing it in the highest band for this strand.

The essay contains all required structural elementsâtitle page with RQ and word count, abstract, contents page, clear section headings, introduction, body, conclusion, bibliographyâarranged logically.  Transitions are smooth and the reader can easily follow the line of thought.  Minor inconsistencies in header formatting do not seriously detract from overall appropriateness, justifying the top band for presentation.

A coherent argument develops through critique and reformulation, with evaluative comments on both Arendt and Fanon.  The student recognises limitations of each position and proposes a nuanced framework.  Evaluation is clearly more than description, yet it lacks sustained, systematic weighing of multiple perspectives and possible objections, so the discussion remains âpartially criticalââsolidly within 7-9, but not reaching the sophistication expected for 10-12.

Primary philosophical sources (Arendt, Fanon) are foregrounded, supported by secondary scholarship, historical case studies, and contemporary news reports.  The selection is appropriate to a 4,000-word EE and demonstrates deliberate choice (e.g., Sharpeville, French Revolution, #MeToo).  While the range could be broaderâother commentators on political violence are absentâthe methods (conceptual analysis plus historical illustration) are well-matched to the question and clearly explained.

The research base is good: primary philosophical texts plus a mix of secondary philosophy and specific historical examples.  Each source is tied back to the RQ, giving it clear relevance.  Nonetheless, the range is not exhaustive (e.g., other theorists of revolutionary violence such as Sorel or Gandhi are absent) so the work sits at the lower end of the 7-9 band rather than approaching âexcellentâ.

The student shows a coherent grasp of Arendtâs distinction between power and violence, her meansâends critique, and Fanonâs âcleansing forceâ thesis.  Concepts are accurately summarised and used to build an independent reformulation.  Occasional oversimplifications (e.g., inevitability of tyrannies collapsing) appear, but the overall philosophical understanding is accurate, structured, and source-based.

The essay offers sustained comparison, identifies weaknesses in Arendtâs predictive scepticism, and constructs a two-condition test for permissible violence.  Conclusions are logically connected to the analysis and not merely asserted.  However, several claims (e.g., inevitability that talk fails under tyranny) are asserted rather than fully demonstrated, and counter-arguments are sometimes acknowledged but not exhaustively weighed, keeping the work in the âgoodâ rather than âexcellentâ range.

Quotations from Arendt are closely analysed; Fanonâs claims are applied directly to counter Arendt; empirical instances (ANC, French Revolution, #MeToo) are used to test theoretical positions.  The material chosen is consistently relevant and generally integrated into the line of reasoning rather than dropped in descriptively.  Some sources (news reports on 6 Jan) are illustrative rather than analytical, preventing a flawless score, but overall application is clearly appropriate.

annotations.comment: The contents page is comprehensive and logically ordered, aiding navigation through sections and subsections effectively.
The research question is articulated prominently and remains central throughout, ensuring clear alignment between enquiry and analysis.
The student meets formatting requirements by providing the word count at the start of the document, confirming adherence to IB guidelines.
The abstract succinctly outlines the line of argument, methodology, and proposed reformulation, offering readers a clear roadmap of the essay.
The abstract claims Arendtâs critique is âphilosophically weakâ without defining evaluation criteria; consider specifying what âstrengthâ entails to improve clarity.
Leverage dot leaders or indentation in the table of contents for enhanced readability and alignment of section titles with page numbers.
Historical context in the introduction (e.g., Sharpeville Massacre) effectively grounds the philosophical debate in real events.
The working definition of violence is simplistic; consider engaging with established philosophical definitions to deepen conceptual clarity.
The distinction between power and violence is introduced clearly with appropriate primaryâtext citations, demonstrating solid knowledge.
When introducing Arendtâs âmeansâend category,â illustrate with a concrete example from the text to anchor the abstract concept.
Quotations are integrated effectively to support analysis rather than mere description, demonstrating strong use of source material.
Expand the discussion of unpredictability by incorporating another historical example to reinforce Arendtâs argument on speculative character.
Integration of the January 6th riot example connects Arendtâs critique to contemporary events, enhancing relevance.
The citation â(Ray & Cunningham, n.d.)â lacks a publication date; verify source details and update accordingly to improve credibility.
The explanation of Arendtâs non-teleological view on speech and power is precise, demonstrating clear conceptual understanding.
To deepen critical evaluation, integrate a counter-perspective from a contemporary scholar on Arendtâs view of violence.
The #MeToo case study vividly illustrates Arendtâs ideal polis in practice, showing effective application of source material.
The assertion that discourse âbecame less tabooâ overstates impact; consider acknowledging ongoing challenges to provide balance.
This subsection concludes Arendtâs argument concisely, reinforcing reader comprehension before transitioning to critique.
Introduce a subheading in this section to clarify the shift from exposition of Arendt to evaluative critique.
The critique on Arendtâs power-violence dichotomy addresses real-world constraints of totalitarian states, showing strong critical thinking.
Embed a direct quotation from Arendtâs response in this critique to anchor your hypothetical reply in primary text.
The expansion of Fanonâs âcleansing forceâ thesis demonstrates excellent integration of complementary theories.
Consider adding scholarly criticism of Fanonâs concept of violence to develop a more balanced evaluation.
The synthesis of Arendt and Fanon in Chapter Reformulating Arendtâs Argument highlights originality and critical engagement.
Explicitly detail the methodology behind source selection in a dedicated section to satisfy Focus and Method criteria.
Reframing violence and power as points on a continuum demonstrates innovative conceptual thinking.
Provide a diagram or visual representation of the power-violence continuum to clarify your model for readers.
The moral-agent framework for activist decision-making adds depth to your reformulation, showing strong critical synthesis.
Include a brief reflection on ethical challenges faced by activists in making violence-permissibility judgments to demonstrate engagement.
The conclusion effectively synthesizes findings and returns to the research question, reinforcing the essayâs coherence.
Enhance the conclusion by summarizing key limitations and suggesting areas for future research to deepen critical reflection.
The bibliography covers a balanced mix of primary texts, secondary analysis, and contemporary sources, demonstrating research breadth.
Consistently format URLs and access dates in the bibliography to ensure a professional and uniform reference list.
The extensive bibliography on pages 20â21 shows thorough effort to locate varied source types beyond core philosophical texts.
Some URLs are excessively long and interrupt layout; consider using shortened DOIs or hyperlink text with full references in footnotes.
Including a contemporary APA blog entry adds relevance and demonstrates engagement with current discourse.
No reflective section is included; add a brief reflection on the research process or decision-making to meet IB engagement criteria.
